ocar.opportunity: The original C. elegans connectome covered the hermaphrodite, but the male worm has a distinct nervous system with sex-specific circuits for mating behavior that had not been fully mapped, limiting understanding of sexually dimorphic neural circuits.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing the complete male C. elegans nervous system required acquiring and annotating a new EM volume covering the additional male-specific neurons in the tail, while maintaining consistency with the female connectome for comparison.
ocar.action: The authors reconstructed the complete connectomes of both C. elegans sexes from volume EM data, tracing all neurons and synapses in both hermaphrodite and male worms to produce a comparative dataset of sexually dimorphic and shared circuits.
ocar.resolution: The dual-sex connectome revealed the circuit architecture of male-specific mating behaviors, identified sexually dimorphic and shared connectivity motifs, and provided a quantitative comparison of how the same core circuits are modified by sex-specific neurons. The dataset updates and completes the original 1986 hermaphrodite connectome.
ocar.future_work: Future work should determine the developmental genetic mechanisms that generate sex-specific circuitry and use the connectome as a blueprint for functional dissection of mating and other sexually dimorphic behaviors.

summaries.beginner: Worms come in two sexes with different nervous systems — the hermaphrodite has 302 nerve cells and the male has more. This study mapped all connections in both the hermaphrodite and male nervous systems, showing exactly how extra nerve cells in the male create different behaviors.
summaries.intermediate: The first complete dual-sex connectome of C. elegans maps all neurons and synapses in both hermaphrodite and male worms, revealing the circuit architecture of male-specific mating behavior and the degree to which shared circuits are modified by sex-specific neurons. The study represents a significant update to the original 1986 data.
summaries.advanced: This paper provides complete revised connectomes for both C. elegans sexes from improved volume EM data, enabling quantitative comparison of shared and sex-specific circuitry. Male-specific neurons (numbering ~200 additional cells) form dedicated mating circuits but also integrate extensively with core locomotion and sensory circuits. Synapse counts and partner identities were updated versus the original White et al. reconstruction, revealing several previously missed connections. Key limitations include the reconstruction representing a single animal per sex and questions about variability across individuals.

discussion_prompts: How do the sex-specific neurons in the C. elegans male integrate with the shared core circuit, and what does this reveal about the evolutionary strategy for building sex-specific behaviors?

What differences exist between the 2019 revised hermaphrodite connectome and the original 1986 White et al. reconstruction, and what do these differences reveal about the limitations of the original data?
How does the complete dual-sex connectome of C. elegans enable comparative approaches to understanding how connectome structure relates to behavioral repertoire across sexes?
